claim: Five-optimization benchmark, complete after Session 19 GPU runs on RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell. (1) Gillespie Rust backend: 1.86x faster than pure Python on the RealSimulator.run hot-path (20-gene sample at production sweep config). KEEP — already integrated. (2) ESM-2 batch-size scan: throughput peaks at bs=16 (247 seq/s); bs=8 is 1.7x slower, bs=32/64 plateau slightly below bs=16 due to padding overhead. The populate notebook already uses bs=16, so no integration change is needed. KEEP_CURRENT_CONFIG. (3) ESM-2 150M vs 650M: 150M is 1.90x faster at inference but loses badly on Tier-1 XGBoost downstream MCC (tier1_only 0.098 vs 0.145; union 0.362 vs 0.443). Both paths remain falsified vs v15's 0.537 baseline; the bottleneck is row count (455), not embedding dim. KEEP_650M. (4) XGBoost gpu_hist vs CPU hist: 2.54x speedup on Tier-1 matrix (455x1295) but absolute savings only 4.45s per CV run; not worth the integration cost. SKIP_INTEGRATION. (5) Feature assembly polars vs pandas+pyarrow: 1.29x mean speedup with high cold-start variance — the matrix is too small for polars to amortize its setup cost. Defer until 10k-row multi-organism scale. Net: of five claimed optimizations, ONE is integrated (Rust) and provides a real speedup; ONE more (bs=16) is already configured optimally; THREE are not worth integrating at the current dataset scale.
source_detail: Reproducible via scripts/bench_rust_vs_python.py (Rust speedup, measured), scripts/bench_xgboost_treemethod.py (XGBoost CPU, measured), scripts/bench_feature_assembly.py (feature assembly, measured), scripts/bench_esm2_batch.py (ESM-2 batch, plan-only in sandbox), scripts/bench_esm2_sizes.py (ESM-2 150M vs 650M, plan-only). Outputs under outputs/bench_*.json. Sandbox hardware: x86_64 Linux, Python 3.11, no CUDA. Seed 42 throughout.

esm2_batch_size_sensitivity.device: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition (95 GiB VRAM)
esm2_batch_size_sensitivity.note: Throughput peaks at bs=16; bs=32 and bs=64 plateau slightly below it (likely padding overhead on the 32-sequence sample). The populate notebook ALREADY uses bs=16 for ESM-2, so the current default is optimal — no integration change needed.

esm2_model_size_comparison.decision_rule_outcome: FAILS — 150M tier1_only MCC 0.098 below 0.12 floor AND union MCC 0.362 below 0.42 floor. Both Tier-1 paths remain falsified, and 150M is strictly worse than 650M on this dataset.
esm2_model_size_comparison.note: Inference is 1.90x faster on 150M but the MCC drop (-0.05 to -0.08 absolute) is too large to justify. The falsified Tier-1 conclusion is robust to model size: row count (455) is the bottleneck, not embedding dimension. Re-evaluate after a multi-organism corpus lands at ~10k rows.

xgboost_tree_method.note: GPU is 2.54x faster than CPU on this matrix — within the published 2-10x range, low end as expected for 455 rows. Absolute savings are only ~4 seconds per CV run; not worth the integration cost (sandbox vs Colab branching, CUDA dep). The MCC delta (CPU 0.270 vs GPU 0.214) is unexpected for the same algorithm and likely reflects a small numerical-binning difference in gpu_hist; another reason to skip until needed at multi-organism scale.

feature_assembly.note: polars shows 1.29x mean speedup but ~5x stdev; the small matrix is in the cold-start regime where polars hasn't amortized its setup cost. On a 10k-row multi-organism matrix the gap is expected to widen in polars' favor.

caveats: All five benchmarks now have measured numbers as of Session 19 (commit 12e2a01). The two GPU-dependent benchmarks (ESM-2 batch + 150M-vs-650M) and the XGBoost GPU branch were measured on a Colab A100-class GPU (RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell, 95 GiB VRAM). The other three were measured in the Claude Code sandbox.

Per-benchmark integration decisions: Rust backend KEEP (already integrated), bs=16 KEEP_CURRENT_CONFIG (already used in populate notebook), 150M model KEEP_650M (loses on MCC despite 1.9x inference speedup), XGBoost GPU SKIP_INTEGRATION (too small absolute savings on 455-row matrix), polars DEFER (cold-start variance too high at this scale). None of the four pending optimizations is being integrated this session.

The Rust speedup of 1.86x is below the 'roughly 2x' claim that prior work reported; re-verified here on a clean 20-gene sample at the same config as the production sweep. It is NOT the 10x that compiled-language rewrites of naive Python typically give, because the current FastEventSimulator is already using numpy-vectorised math and the remaining hot-path is limited by stochastic rule-sampling overhead rather than arithmetic.

The feature-assembly benchmark uses synthetic parquets (same shape + NaN pattern as Tier-1) rather than the real cache files, so we can iterate 10x without repeated disk-cache effects. Real-cache numbers would show higher variance due to filesystem caching.

XGBoost CV MCC measured here (0.2405) differs from the main train_tier1_xgboost.py result (0.1452) for the tier1 config; the main script uses the exact same hyperparameters and 5-fold split so the discrepancy likely comes from a small difference in how NaN is handled in this benchmark's path (no explicit label vector construction call). This file should NOT be treated as a replacement for the headline MCC measurements — it is a training-time benchmark only.
